activities_projects.title: Humour in Social Blunders
activities_projects.objective: Identify how the author uses 'awkwardness' to create wit
steps: Find a specific scene where a character says the 'wrong' thing by mistake.
Explain in 50 words why the situation is funny (e.g., Is it a misunderstanding or a slip of the tongue?).
Discuss with a partner: How does the character's reaction add to the humor?
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students analyze situational irony and the role of embarrassment in humor.
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of explanation and identification of humorous elements (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Art of the Witty Comeback
activities_projects.objective: Practice responding to rude or awkward comments with wit
steps: Identify a moment in the text where a character uses a clever or funny reply to fix a situation.
Write three original 'Witty Comebacks' for common awkward school situations (e.g., someone forgets your name).
Ensure the replies are polite but clever, keeping with the 'Right Thing' theme.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students develop creative thinking and verbal dexterity.
activities_projects.evaluation: Creativity, politeness, and relevance of the comebacks (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Vocabulary: Nuance in Manners
activities_projects.objective: Distinguish between similar social verbs and adjectives
steps: Find the words 'Apologize', 'Regret', 'Confess', and 'Acknowledge' in the text.
Research the subtle differences in their meanings.
Write a separate sentence for each word showing you understand when to use which one.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students master the precision of language in social interactions.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct contextual usage of the selected vocabulary (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Character Analysis: The Social Mentor
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the character who guides others in the story
steps: Identify the character who usually knows the 'Right Thing' to say.
List three character traits that make them a good social guide (e.g., patience, quick wit).
Write a short paragraph (80 words) describing how this character uses humor to teach others.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students practice characterization and evidence-based writing.
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of analysis and use of textual evidence (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Scripting a Polite 'No'
activities_projects.objective: Practice the difficult art of refusing politely
steps: Choose a scene where a character is pressured into doing something they don't want to do.
Rewrite the dialogue to show the character saying 'No' in a way that is firm yet funny and polite.
Perform the dialogue with a classmate focusing on tone of voice.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students learn to set boundaries using polite and witty language.
activities_projects.evaluation: Effectiveness of the refusal and tone of the script (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Grammar: Reported Speech and Social Context
activities_projects.objective: Transform witty exchanges into formal reports
steps: Identify a dialogue involving more than two people in the chapter.
Convert the exchange into a formal narrative report (Indirect Speech).
Make sure to describe the *manner* in which things were said (e.g., 'He remarked jokingly', 'She replied sheepishly').
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students practice narration and the use of reporting verbs.
activities_projects.evaluation: Grammatical accuracy and appropriate use of reporting verbs (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'Politeness' Paradox
activities_projects.objective: Explore when being too polite can become funny
steps: Find a moment where excessive politeness leads to a funny or confusing situation.
Write a short satirical paragraph about a character who is *so* polite they can't even finish a sentence.
Explain why balance is important in communication.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students understand the concept of satire and social balance.
activities_projects.evaluation: Creativity and understanding of the satirical tone (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Visualizing Idioms
activities_projects.objective: Understand common social idioms from the text
steps: Identify social idioms used in the chapter (e.g., 'Break the ice', 'Put your foot in it').
Draw a literal version of the idiom on one side and its actual meaning on the other.
Write a sentence for each idiom based on the story's plot.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students learn the figurative meaning of common social idioms.
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct interpretation and creative illustration (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Cultural Comparison: Manners
activities_projects.objective: Relate the story's etiquette to different cultures
steps: Identify one 'Right Thing' from the book (like a specific greeting).
Research how that same greeting or social action is done in a different country.
Write a 100-word comparative piece on how the 'Right Thing' changes based on where you are.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students develop global awareness and cultural empathy.
activities_projects.evaluation: Research accuracy and clarity of comparison (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'Social Rescue' Case Study
activities_projects.objective: Apply the chapter's lessons to problem-solving
steps: The teacher provides a 'Social Emergency' (e.g., you accidentally insulted someone's cooking).
Using the 'Wit and Humour' approach from the book, write a three-step plan to fix the situation.
Present your 'Rescue Plan' to the class.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students practice critical thinking and social problem-solving.
activities_projects.evaluation: Logic, humor, and effectiveness of the solution (10 marks)
